title: Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group
subtitle: Estimated annual number of deaths by age group from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people in the same age group. These include alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders. Figures do not include deaths resulting from suicide, which are related to these disorders.
caption:
Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group

Estimated annual number of deaths by age group from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people in the same age group. These include alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders. Figures do not include deaths resulting from suicide, which are related to these disorders.

The horizontal bar chart displays the estimated annual number of deaths per 100,000 people attributed to mental health and substance use disorders across six distinct age groups for the year 2021. The data reveals a non-linear distribution where mortality rates remain negligible during early life stages before rising significantly in adulthood and peaking in older demographics. For the youngest cohorts, the 0-4 years age group records a rate of 0, while the 5-14 years group shows a value of less than 0.1 deaths per 100,000 people. Moving into the working-age population, the rate for the 15-49 years group increases to 4.5, a figure nearly identical to the aggregate rate for all ages, which stands at 4.4. The highest mortality burdens appear in the older brackets, with the 70+ years group registering a rate of 6.9 deaths per 100,000 people. The maximum value on the chart belongs to the 50-69 years age group, which exhibits the highest death rate at 9.2 per 100,000 people, surpassing the rate of the oldest demographic by more than two points. The visual arrangement highlights that the risk of death from these disorders is concentrated almost entirely within the population aged 15 and above, with the most severe impact occurring between the ages of 50 and 69.
